Not only were they often links to the purchase pages for software I'd already paid for, downloaded, and installed, but they were the first results in the list. If that wasn't bad enough, sometimes the search didn't even return the link to the app I knew was installed.

Even Windows ME was more user-friendly, and that crashed if you looked at it funny.

I can only imagine this is some scheme to increase Bing search user numbers, because it's one of the worst user experiences I've ever had in all of my years of computing. Even Windows ME was more user-friendly, and that crashed if you looked at it funny. Maybe it's also a way to push users to use the Search icon that sits on the taskbar next to the Start menu, but that feels like duplicating a function for the sake of it.

Thankfully, there's a way to disable it, but Microsoft doesn't make it easy to find. In fact, you probably won't find it if you look unless you follow an online guide like this one to disable Bing search from our sister site, MakeUseOf. It does require a little bit of registry editing, but it won't take long, and then your Start menu will be (mostly) functional again when you search for things.

 Related
How to turn off Start menu recommended content in Windows 11

The Windows 11 Start menu has a new section for recommended content, but it may not be useful for everyone. Here's how you can turn it off.

5 Adding new buttons to the taskbar

Only I should be able to do this

Windows has a habit of putting app icons on the taskbar when new features are introduced, like the new Copilot icon or the Widgets icon. Now, it's not the ability to have the icon on the taskbar that I take issue with. Some things are just fine, like the Task View button that shows you every open app window and your virtual desktops. It's that Microsoft adds them to the taskbar in the first place as the default setting and then makes you opt-out.

It's a common theme in the tech world: features are pushed onto users, and then it's on them to opt-out if they don't want to use them. I don't want Copilot to be added to this list without my acceptance, and it annoys me that the default for Windows is to assume the user will want to see the new features. It underscores that even if you paid for your Windows license, you don't really own it. Microsoft does, and they will add whatever they want to the screen you see daily.

Related
How to remove icons from the taskbar in Windows 11

If apps are cluttering your Windows 11 taskbar, it’s time to remove a few icons

4 Offloading support documents to the web

I'd use Google if I wanted web results, thanks

​​​​​​​

But the link to the online resources doesn't even directly hyperlink to the page. Instead, it opens Bing in Edge (even if you have another browser as default) and searches for a relevant phrase on "site:microsoft.com." It's another way of pushing traffic to another Microsoft service that nobody really uses. I could maybe see the point in not bloating the Windows install further with support documents, except those documents are mostly text, which takes up barely any storage space.

item.title: 6 things your home lab needs to stay pristine (and you probably forgot)
item.itunes:title: 6 things your home lab needs to stay pristine (and you probably forgot)
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Setting up a home lab is one task, but keeping it efficient and well-maintained is an entirely separate challenge. With tangled wires, dust, increased temperatures, and an ever-growing collection of hardware, it doesn't take long for your home lab to turn into chaos. It affects your productivity, damages equipment, leads to data loss, and ultimately spoils your digital sanctuary.

In this post, I will go over the top seven tips to keep your home lab in pristine condition, tackling clutter, ensuring optimal performance, and extending its lifespan.

 Related
I built a Proxmox home lab using my Raspberry Pi - here's how I did it

Who says you need a full-fledged server PC to tinker with Proxmox?

6

6 Control temperature and humidity levels

High temperatures and humidity are two of the biggest silent killers in your home lab. Over time, excessive heat can cause components to degrade, malfunction, and ultimately fail. There are several ways to keep the rising temperature in check in your home lab.

You can buy some cooling solutions like a room AC, place your fans strategically to improve airflow, dissipate generated heat, and ensure a constant flow of fresh air. If your home lab consists of high-performance servers, make sure to invest in coolants like heatsinks and liquid cooling systems.

If you live in a humid climate, use a dehumidifier to maintain optimal humidity levels. After all, high humidity can cause corrosion, short circuits, and damage sensitive electronic components. Overall, managing temperature and humidity creates a stable and safe environment for pricey home lab hardware.

5 Have proper cable management

Cable management is the unsung hero of a pristine home lab. Aside from making your home lab more tidy and easy on the eyes, proper cable management also creates a safe and efficient work environment. Here is how you can achieve cable nirvana.

Before you build your home lab, plan your layout carefully. You need to think about cable routes and access points and get some cable ties, organizers, and labels to bundle cables together and secure them to surfaces.

Cable management is an ongoing process and requires regular maintenance and adjustments. The idea is to not let things get out of hand!

Efficient cable management prevents overheating, gives you easy access, and prevents dust buildup and accidents.

 Related
5 of the best accessories for your home lab

Take your home lab setup to the next level by investing in these five useful devices

1

4 Keep your home lab components dust-free

While dust may seem harmless at first glance, those tiny particles can create chaos in your home lab in no time. Dust buildup can result in overheating, component malfunction, and even system failure. However, fret not, taking care of dust isn't rocket science.

Simply create a routine of cleaning your home lab regularly. You can use compressed air to blow out dust from hard-to-reach areas and a vacuum cleaner to clean floors and other surface areas. You can also use dust covers, keep windows closed, and avoid any activities that generate dust or debris.

3 Ensure sufficient power protection

Source: Amazon

Your home lab has multiple components and equipment running simultaneously. If you have miscalculated your power requirements or live in an area where power fluctuates a lot, sudden voltage spikes can damage electrical components. Such a situation can interrupt your work, cause data loss, and lead to costly repairs.

You can pick a UPS with surge protection that fits your needs and budget, ensure proper electrical grounding, and install Ground Fault Circuit Interrupters (GFCI) in moist areas.

 Related
7 things you should know before building your first home lab

Your essential home lab checklist to avoid costly mistakes

1

2 Protect your data with regular backups

When you experiment with different software solutions, hardware components, and home lab OS, it can corrupt data. Data backups are non-negotiable in any home lab. After all, a corrupted hard drive, accidental deletion, or a system failure due to the factors mentioned above can affect your ongoing projects, waste countless hours, and even cost you money.

You need to have a backup strategy to keep copies of your important data in external hard drives, NAS, and cloud storage. Such a practice protects your data, acts as a recovery during a disaster, and gives you some much-needed peace of mind. You should also protect your confidential data with encryption to prevent unauthorized access.

 Related
4 reasons why every PC enthusiast should build a home lab

Fair warning: you might get addicted to creating home lab projects if you're not careful...

1

1 Ensure regular firmware and software updates

This is a crucial element for maintaining your home lab in peak working condition. Whether you use popular OS or open-source software, it’s important to stay updated to the latest version. Developers frequently release new firmware to add new features, security, and bug fixes. You are basically leaving your home lab to security threats and cyberattacks when you skip such updates.

You should also update your applications and device drivers to ensure compatibility and optimal performance.

Maximize your home lab’s potential

By following our recommendations, you will not only elevate your home lab’s performance, efficiency, and lifespan but also develop a more productive setup for work and experimentation. After all, your home lab isn’t just a launchpad for learning; it is a reflection of your passion for technology that needs regular optimizations and checkups.

What are you waiting for? Follow the above-mentioned routine and keep your home lab running smoothly without breaking a sweat. If you plan to expand your home lab with additional hardware, check out my tips to keep your budget in check.

1 Ensure regular firmware and software updates

This is a crucial element for maintaining your home lab in peak working condition. Whether you use popular OS or open-source software, it’s important to stay updated to the latest version. Developers frequently release new firmware to add new features, security, and bug fixes. You are basically leaving your home lab to security threats and cyberattacks when you skip such updates.

You should also update your applications and device drivers to ensure compatibility and optimal performance.

Maximize your home lab’s potential

By following our recommendations, you will not only elevate your home lab’s performance, efficiency, and lifespan but also develop a more productive setup for work and experimentation. After all, your home lab isn’t just a launchpad for learning; it is a reflection of your passion for technology that needs regular optimizations and checkups.

What are you waiting for? Follow the above-mentioned routine and keep your home lab running smoothly without breaking a sweat. If you plan to expand your home lab with additional hardware, check out my tips to keep your budget in check.
